issues.title: README knowledge-file count left out of sync: still says "nine files" after analysis.md became the 10th
issues.description: README.md:348 — "**`.gogo/knowledge/`** — your project's configuration: the nine files described in [Generic flow, your rules] above." The referenced table (README.md:62-73) was correctly updated to include the new `analysis.md` row and now lists TEN files, and docs/architecture.md was updated in BOTH spots (line 122 "the 10 knowledge-file scaffolds", line 157 "your config — 10 files"). But this prose count in README was missed, so it now contradicts its own table. This is exactly the enumeration-sync class the project's own standards call out: code-review-standards.md §1 and coding-rules.md ("Keep enumerations in sync ... README.md. Grep before you finish."). Per the severity guide, "an enumeration left out of sync" is a Major.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. In README.md change "the nine files described in" to "the ten files described in". Then grep the repo once more for any other bare 'nine'/'9 files' knowledge-count references.
issues.fix_summary: README:359 nine->ten; repo-wide grep clean of stale counts. [orchestrator-fixed inline + verified round 2]

issues.title: "commands/* orchestrator-first sweep" only reached plan.md + go.md; the standalone phase commands were not touched
issues.description: Plan Stage-A checklist item 4 scopes the sweep as "skills/gogo/SKILL.md + commands/* orchestrator-first sweep", and FR3 asks every command's docs to state the architecture uniformly. commands/plan.md and commands/go.md carried the explicit orchestrator-first sentence and docs/commands.md carries it at the group level, but commands/implement.md and commands/report.md carried no delegation framing. No CONTRADICTION existed, so this was a completeness/uniformity gap, not a behavioural defect.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (optional). Add the one-line orchestrator-first note to commands/implement.md and commands/report.md so all command files read uniformly.
issues.fix_summary: Uniform orchestrator one-liner appended to commands/implement.md + commands/report.md. [orchestrator-fixed inline + verified round 2]

issues.title: commands/report.md still instructs "set state.md to done" — the pre-UAT status, contradicting the new awaiting-uat gate
issues.description: commands/report.md:45-46 (validate-out step): "write report/manifest.json + report/result.json; set `state.md` to done." This describes the OLD behaviour. The whole feature reframes phase ⑤: on a green run gogo-knowledge/SKILL.md now sets `phase: done, status: awaiting-uat` (never `done`), and the parallel prose in docs/commands.md:141-143 WAS deliberately updated to "sets `state.md` to **`awaiting-uat`** (the UAT gate — no longer `done`)". commands/report.md is the one command-reference summary the sweep missed, so an official command doc still tells the reader /gogo:report leaves a green feature at `done`, skipping UAT. This is the cross-file-consistency / "no place still describes the old behaviour" class (code-review-standards.md §1); same class as REV-001. (Note: `phase` IS still set to done, so the line is literally half-true, which is exactly why it is misleading.)
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Change commands/report.md:46 to match gogo-knowledge + docs/commands.md, e.g. "set `state.md` to **`awaiting-uat`** (the UAT gate — no longer `done`; a lenient past/broken run leaves the real pre-report status)."
issues.fix_summary: commands/report.md:45-46 validate-out now sets `state.md` to **awaiting-uat** (the UAT gate — no longer done; lenient past/broken run leaves the real pre-report status), matching gogo-knowledge + docs/commands.md. [orchestrator-verified: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]

issues.title: UAT loop never moves state.md out of `awaiting-uat` during a re-plan — leaves a mid-loop feature classified ready-to-ship and ship-able before re-acceptance
issues.description: gogo/SKILL.md UAT loop (lines 191-207): step 1 emits `uat-opened` and sets only `resume: report`; the STATUS stays `awaiting-uat` while gogo-analyst rewrites plan.md (step 2), and it flips to `plan-accepted` only at step 3 (on the user's re-acceptance). So throughout the re-plan window state.md holds `awaiting-uat` with a revised, un-re-accepted, un-re-implemented plan.md. This is the same value ⑤ leaves on first arrival, so nothing distinguishes "awaiting the user's first verdict" from "mid-re-plan, awaiting re-acceptance." Consequences, all reachable via the multi-entry-point design (state.md is the durable resume file; the CLI board shows an `awaiting-uat` badge and can launch /gogo:done or /gogo:go on that card): (i) gogo-status classifies the feature ready-to-ship during a re-plan (awaiting-uat + report present); (ii) /gogo:done validate-in accepts on `awaiting-uat`, so a done invoked mid-loop would ship the revised-but-unimplemented plan — the shipped report.md/plan.md would then describe a plan the code does not implement, bypassing re-acceptance AND re-implementation; (iii) go.md:14-15,19-21 admits a "resumable in-loop state" / "mid-loop" as an alternative to `plan-accepted`, and `awaiting-uat` is undefined w.r.t. that clause — a direct /gogo:go could rerun the revised plan without re-acceptance. This contradicts the D1 invariant (the accept branch and the re-plan branch must be mutually exclusive) and code-review-standards.md §5 ("state.md is kept current at transitions"). The established decision-gate pattern (gogo-review sets `waiting-for-user` on entry) is NOT followed here. Happy path (one continuous in-chat session) is safe; the gap is an interrupted / out-of-band invocation, which the durable-state + board design makes real.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (recommended pattern established; exact intermediate status is a small design call). When the UAT loop opens (step 1), set `state.md` `status: waiting-for-user` (mirroring the decision-gate pattern) with `resume: report` — keep it there through the analyst round and the re-acceptance wait, then flip to `plan-accepted` on acceptance (step 3). That makes /gogo:done's `awaiting-uat` validate-in refuse a mid-re-plan feature, keeps the classifier from showing ready-to-ship during a re-plan, and removes the `awaiting-uat`-as-resumable-in-loop ambiguity in go.md. Optionally state explicitly in go.md that `awaiting-uat` is NOT a resumable-in-loop state for /gogo:go (re-acceptance is required first).
issues.fix_summary: Mid-UAT lock (per decisions.md 'REV-004 mid-UAT status'): the moment the user raises UAT issues the orchestrator sets `state.md` status=waiting-for-user (open-decision: UAT round N, resume: plan) BEFORE delegating to the analyst, and holds it there through analysis + plan revision + re-presentation; only user re-acceptance flips it to plan-accepted. Written into gogo/SKILL.md UAT loop (step 1 lock + step 3), agents/gogo-analyst.md second-job (intro + step 4), docs/flow.md UAT section, docs/commands.md (/gogo:go reads), templates/state.template.md comment. Consequence stated explicitly: gogo-done validate-in REFUSES a report-complete member at waiting-for-user (mid-re-plan cannot ship); commands/go.md + docs/commands.md go gate EXCLUDE awaiting-uat AND waiting-for-user from the runnable/'resumable in-loop' set — awaiting-uat is the user's UAT gate, only plan-accepted (re)runs the pipeline. uat-opened event status updated awaiting-uat→waiting-for-user. [orchestrator-verified round 3: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]. Round 3 CLI spot-check: model.go badge() precedence implements the same rule (waiting-for-user wins the mid-UAT badge over awaiting-uat), tested in TestBadgeAwaitingUAT — the 0.11.0 CLI and the contract agree.

issues.title: UAT re-acceptance tells the orchestrator to emit `plan-accepted`, contradicting the single-owner-per-event contract (owner = gogo-plan)
issues.description: gogo/SKILL.md UAT step 3 (line 202): "On acceptance: ... **append the plan's `plan-accepted` event**, and emit `uat-failed`." But three places pin `plan-accepted` to gogo-plan alone: (a) the SAME file's ownership summary (gogo/SKILL.md:279-282) says "As the orchestrator you emit **only** `gate-opened`/`gate-resolved` ... **and the two UAT-loop events** `uat-opened`/`uat-failed`" — plan-accepted is not in that list; (b) docs/cli-contract.md:220 owner table: `plan-accepted`/plan → owner `gogo-plan`; (c) skills/gogo-plan/SKILL.md:129-131: "`plan-accepted` is the plan phase's terminal event — this skill owns both plan events ... the orchestrator emits none." The documented invariant (cli-contract §5) is "each transition is emitted exactly once, by its owning skill — no event is written by two owners." Assigning plan-accepted to the orchestrator in the UAT path creates a second owner / an intra-file contradiction. Low functional impact (telemetry; the CLI parses leniently) but a real contract-consistency defect (code-review-standards.md §1 + §7).
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Cleanest: drop "append the plan's `plan-accepted` event" from UAT step 3 — `uat-failed` already marks the re-accept-and-rerun, and the state.md status still flips to plan-accepted. Alternatively, keep it and reconcile the docs: extend gogo/SKILL.md:279 and the cli-contract §5 owner table + gogo-plan.md note to say plan-accepted may also be emitted by the orchestrator on a UAT re-acceptance.
issues.fix_summary: gogo/SKILL.md UAT step 3 no longer has the orchestrator 'append the plan's plan-accepted event'. The re-acceptance now lands through the normal plan-acceptance flow whose plan-accepted event is OWNED by gogo-plan (single owner preserved); the orchestrator emits ONLY uat-failed (and bumps iterations uat=N). Ownership line + gogo-analyst note reinforce the single owner. [orchestrator-verified: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]

issues.title: docs/architecture.md pipeline one-liner still ends "⑤ REPORT → done" — the only flow depiction not updated for the UAT gate
issues.description: docs/architecture.md:18 (the canonical five-phase diagram in §1): "goal → ① PLAN → ② IMPLEMENT → ③ REVIEW → ④ TEST → ⑤ REPORT → done". Every OTHER flow depiction was updated to show the UAT gate before ship: skills/gogo/SKILL.md:95 (⑤ REPORT → UAT gate → shipped), docs/flow.md:20-22 mermaid (REP =awaiting-uat=> UAT =/gogo:done accepts=> shipped), docs/index.md, and README. This top-of-doc summary was left stale, so architecture.md contradicts flow.md/index.md/the skill. This is the doc-sync-sweep-must-enumerate-all-docs class (code-review-standards.md §1) — the same completeness gap REV-001 caught. Coarse (it shows no status value), hence Minor rather than Major.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Update docs/architecture.md:18 to reflect the UAT gate, e.g. "goal → ① PLAN → ② IMPLEMENT → ③ REVIEW → ④ TEST → ⑤ REPORT → UAT gate → shipped" (and/or a one-line note that ⑤ ends at awaiting-uat).
issues.fix_summary: docs/architecture.md:18 pipeline one-liner updated 'goal → ① PLAN → ② IMPLEMENT → ③ REVIEW → ④ TEST → ⑤ REPORT → UAT gate → shipped' (matches flow.md/index.md/the skill). [orchestrator-verified: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]

issues.title: /gogo:skills can flag/extract a `## Custom` section — gogo-skills was not updated to exempt it the way it exempts `## gogo overrides`
issues.description: This feature installs an identical `## Custom` stub into all 20 knowledge files (10 templates + 10 .gogo) whose comment promises unconditionally: "gogo never rewrites this section." But skills/gogo-skills/SKILL.md (untouched by this feature — confirmed `git diff --stat` empty) still discovers extraction candidates without exempting `## Custom`: Step 2 (line 52-53) says "Never flag the `gogo:meta` header or a `## gogo overrides` section" — `## Custom` is absent from that list — and Step 1/Budget (line 30) counts ALL body lines. So a user's `## Custom` section ≥20 lines in a WARN/OVER file could be proposed for extraction and, on approval, replaced by a stub pointer — gogo rewriting a section the file swears it never touches. Mitigations that keep this below blocker: extraction is user-gated (Step 4 STOPs for per-candidate approval), and FR5's literal "never rewritten" scope named only /gogo:build + phase-⑤ (not /gogo:skills). Still a real cross-file consistency gap given the unconditional file-level promise and question (d) of the review brief.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Mirror the `## gogo overrides` treatment in skills/gogo-skills/SKILL.md: add `## Custom` to Step 2's never-flag list, and exclude `## Custom` bodies from the Budget line-count in Step 1/Budget (it is user-owned, not gogo's always-read context) so a large Custom section never trips the over-budget nudge or becomes a candidate. Was gogo-skills in the Stage B scope? — if intentionally deferred, record that decision; otherwise close this gap so the stub's promise holds across all commands.
issues.fix_summary: skills/gogo-skills/SKILL.md exempts `## Custom` exactly like `## gogo overrides`: excluded from the Step 1/Budget line count AND from Step 2 candidate discovery (never flagged, never proposed, never extracted). Noted where the budget is described: docs/commands.md /gogo:skills section + docs/architecture.md §2 budget paragraph. [orchestrator-verified: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]

issues.title: commands/report.md orchestrator note has garbled nested-parens wording and implies ⑤ is agent-delegated
issues.description: commands/report.md:48-49 (added this round): "Like every gogo command, this invokes the **orchestrator**, which delegates the phase to its specialist (gogo-knowledge (run by the orchestrator)) and owns any gates in chat." The nested parens "(gogo-knowledge (run by the orchestrator))" read awkwardly, and ⑤ report is NOT delegated to a specialist AGENT — everywhere else (gogo/SKILL.md, docs/commands.md, agents.md) it is described as "⑤ orchestrator + gogo-knowledge" / "run by the orchestrator via gogo-knowledge". The parenthetical tries to self-correct the specialist framing but ends up self-contradictory. Compare the clean parallel note in commands/implement.md ("its specialist (gogo-developer)") which is correct because ② genuinely delegates to an agent.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Reword commands/report.md:48-49 to match the house phrasing, e.g. "Like every gogo command, this invokes the **orchestrator**; phase ⑤ the orchestrator runs itself via the `gogo-knowledge` skill (the other phases delegate to a specialist agent), and it owns any gates in chat."
issues.fix_summary: commands/report.md:48-49 reworded to the house phrasing: 'this invokes the orchestrator; phase ⑤ the orchestrator runs itself via the gogo-knowledge skill (the other phases delegate to a specialist agent), and it owns any gates in chat.' Garbled nested parens removed; ⑤ no longer implied to be agent-delegated. [orchestrator-verified: status-trace shows one legal command per state; greps clean; contracts validate]

issues.title: EXDEV cross-device trash fallback (copyTree) follows symlinks via os.Stat — dereferences links and can infinite-recurse on a symlink cycle
issues.description: cli/internal/trash/trash.go:257-278 copyTree (the only path taken when os.Rename fails with EXDEV, i.e. .gogo/trash is on a different filesystem than .gogo/work) resolves each entry with `os.Stat(src)`, which FOLLOWS symlinks. Two consequences in the fallback: (1) a symlink inside a work folder is copied as a dereferenced regular file/dir instead of being preserved as a link — the trashed copy is not byte-faithful (the doc comment claims only 'preserving file modes', so this is a fidelity gap, not a contradiction); (2) a symlink that points at an ancestor forms a cycle — os.Stat reports IsDir()=true, copyTree recurses into it, and there is no visited-set or depth bound, so it recurses until the goroutine stack overflows and the process crashes (coding-rules 'never crash'). Severity is a nit because reachability is very low: it needs BOTH a cross-device trash mount AND a symlink (worse, a cyclic one) inside a gogo-authored work folder, which never contains symlinks in normal operation. The same-device os.Rename happy path (the overwhelmingly common case) is unaffected. Data-safety of the fallback is otherwise intact — the source is only removed after a successful copy (moveDir:238-242), and a copy error removes the partial DEST, never the source.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (defensive hardening of the rarely-hit fallback). In copyTree use `os.Lstat` instead of `os.Stat` and handle `ModeSymlink` explicitly — recreate the link with os.Symlink (faithful) or skip it — so a symlink is never dereferenced and a cycle can never drive unbounded recursion. Optionally add a depth guard as belt-and-suspenders. A unit test with a symlink entry (and a self-referential one) would pin it, though EXDEV itself cannot be forced portably.
issues.fix_summary: cli/internal/trash/trash.go copyTree now uses os.Lstat (not os.Stat) and handles ModeSymlink explicitly: a symlink is recreated as a link via os.Readlink+os.Symlink and NEVER followed — byte-faithful, and a cyclic link (points at an ancestor) is copied as the link itself so no recursion/stack-overflow is possible. Doc comment updated to say it preserves symlinks. New cycle-proof test TestCopyTreeSymlinkNoRecurse builds src/sub/loop -> .. (self-referential) plus a sibling link, runs copyTree, and asserts it completes without recursing, that both links are preserved as symlinks with their original targets, and the real file still copies. gofmt/vet/-race green. [orchestrator-verified: -race green incl. the 5 new tests (cycle-proof symlink, outside-work refusal, suffix parse, double-trash, Esc-preserves-selection)]

issues.title: MoveToTrash has no package-level guard that its target is a .gogo/work feature folder — the 'changelog is never deletable' invariant rests solely on the single TUI column check
issues.description: Decision D3 / FR6 make the changelog an append-only archive that must never be trashed. In the shipped code that invariant is enforced in exactly ONE place: cli/internal/tui/delete.go:19-22 bounces when `f.Column() == contract.ColChangelog`. The destructive package API cli/internal/trash/trash.go:62 MoveToTrash(root, featureDir) itself does NO validation — it will move ANY directory handed to it (slugFromFeatureDir just TrimPrefix'es 'feature-', tolerating a base that lacks the prefix). Today this is safe by construction, not by defense in depth: (a) MoveToTrash has a single caller (grep-confirmed: delete.go:58) and that caller is guarded; (b) the changelog ARCHIVE dirs under .gogo/changelog/ are never Features and never surface as board cards, and a shipped card's `Dir` points at its .gogo/work/feature-* folder, not the archive — so no card can hand a changelog-archive path to MoveToTrash. The gap is purely belt-and-suspenders: a future second caller, or a refactor that lets a card's Dir drift, would trash whatever path it received with no invariant to stop it. The review brief explicitly asks for defense-in-depth on this path.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE (optional hardening). Add a cheap precondition to MoveToTrash: reject when filepath.Base(featureDir) does not start with the 'feature-' prefix, and/or assert the resolved path is under filepath.Join(root, ".gogo", "work") (never under .gogo/changelog). Return a clear error rather than moving. That makes the changelog-not-deletable invariant hold at the package boundary, independent of the single UI check. Alternatively, record that the UI-only guard is a deliberate, sufficient choice.
issues.fix_summary: MoveToTrash gained a package-level defense (new requireUnderWork helper): it resolves the source to an absolute path and, via filepath.Rel against <root>/.gogo/work, refuses (clear error) anything that is not under .gogo/work/ — so a .gogo/changelog/... path can never be moved regardless of caller. The TUI ColChangelog bounce stays; this is the belt-and-suspenders the brief asked for, holding the append-only-changelog invariant (D3/FR6) at the package boundary. New test TestMoveToTrashRefusesOutsideWork attempts to trash a .gogo/changelog/2026-07-04-... folder, asserts MoveToTrash errors, the changelog dir is untouched, and List shows no entry created. gofmt/vet/-race green. [orchestrator-verified: -race green incl. the 5 new tests (cycle-proof symlink, outside-work refusal, suffix parse, double-trash, Esc-preserves-selection)]

issues.title: uniqueDest's -N collision suffix leaks into the parsed slug, so a same-second re-delete restores under a wrong folder name
issues.description: cli/internal/trash/trash.go:214-226 uniqueDest disambiguates a same-second re-delete of an identically-named slug by appending '-2','-3',... to the trash entry base, yielding e.g. '20260704T024500Z-my-slug-2'. But parseBase (trash.go:168-173) splits the base on the FIRST '-' to separate the compact timestamp from the slug, so it parses that base's slug token as 'my-slug-2' — the disambiguator becomes part of the slug. Consequently Entry.Slug='my-slug-2' and Entry.OrigName='feature-my-slug-2', and Restore (trash.go:115-136) recreates the folder at .gogo/work/feature-my-slug-2 instead of feature-my-slug. Reachability is extremely low (nit): it requires TWO deletes of the exact same slug within the same UTC second, which the board cannot produce at human speed (after the first delete the slug is gone from .gogo/work; a second identically-named folder would have to be created and deleted inside one second). So this is a latent correctness bug in defensive code, not a practical one. No data loss — the entry is still listed and restorable, just under a name with an extra '-N'.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Either (a) store the original slug on the Entry independently of the base name (keep the pre-uniqueDest slug and use it for OrigName/Restore), or (b) widen tsLayout to sub-second precision so a same-second collision is essentially impossible and uniqueDest rarely fires, or (c) place the counter before the first '-' boundary so parseBase keeps the true slug. A unit test that deletes the same slug twice at a pinned time would pin the restore name.
issues.fix_summary: Made the collision suffix parse-safe by changing its separator from '-' to '.': uniqueDest now appends '.2','.3',... (e.g. <ts>-<slug>.2). parseBase strips a trailing '.N' (new stripCollisionSuffix helper: only when N is all digits, so a legit dotted slug is left intact) after the first-'-' split, so the parsed slug — and thus Entry.OrigName and Restore — is always the exact original feature name; the counter never leaks. Two tests: TestCollisionSuffixParseSafe (uniqueDest yields '.2', parseBase/entryFor recover feature-my-slug, a non-numeric dotted slug is preserved) and end-to-end TestSameSecondDoubleTrashRestores (double-trash the same slug -> both entries carry feature-my-slug; restoring the first recreates it and restoring the second REFUSES on the name collision as designed). gofmt/vet/-race green. [orchestrator-verified: -race green incl. the 5 new tests (cycle-proof symlink, outside-work refusal, suffix parse, double-trash, Esc-preserves-selection)]

issues.title: Esc-aborting a DELETE confirm clears the ready-to-ship multi-selection as a side effect (inconsistent with the Cancel-button path)
issues.description: cli/internal/tui/update.go: the two ways to cancel a delete confirm dispose of state differently. Pressing Esc routes through updateForm's Esc intercept (update.go:326-328) → cancelForm (update.go:363-373), which unconditionally resets `m.selected = map[string]bool{}` (plus pending/pendingShip/pendingDelete). Picking the 'Cancel' button and submitting instead reaches huh.StateCompleted with pendingDelete!=nil → finishDelete (delete.go:47-57), which on !confirmed clears only the delete state and LEAVES `m.selected` intact. So a user who has space-selected several ready cards, then focuses any card and presses `x`, then hits Esc, silently loses the whole ready-ship selection; the same abort via the Cancel button keeps it. cancelForm's rationale ('so a stale unconfirmed target can never be re-shipped by a later m/d', update.go:361-362) is about LAUNCH forms — for a delete form the ship-selection is unrelated, so wiping it is an unintended cross-path inconsistency. Nit: cosmetic/UX, no data or state-machine impact, and the selection is trivially re-made.
issues.proposed_solution: AGENT-FIXABLE. Make the two cancel paths consistent for a delete. Simplest: in cancelForm, only clear the ship selection when a launch was in flight (e.g. guard the `m.selected` reset on `m.pendingDelete == nil`), so aborting a delete leaves the ready selection untouched — matching the Cancel-button (finishDelete) behaviour. A small model-level test that selects a ready card, opens+Esc's a delete, and asserts the selection survives would pin it.
issues.fix_summary: Aligned both cancel paths through one func with a preserveSelection distinction: cancelForm now takes a preserveSelection bool — for a SHIP form (false) it still clears m.selected/pending/pendingShip (TEST-002 rule), for a DELETE form (true) it clears only pending delete + form state and PRESERVES the ready-ship selection. All three call sites (Esc intercept, StateCompleted-cancel, StateAborted) pass `m.pendingDelete != nil`, so Esc on a delete confirm now behaves exactly like the Cancel button (finishDelete). New test TestDeleteCancelPreservesSelection exercises BOTH delete-cancel routes (Esc and the 'n'/Cancel button) and asserts the 2-card ready selection survives each; the existing SHIP-form clear is still pinned by TestFormEscAborts / TestFormAbortClearsSelectionNoRelaunch. gofmt/vet/-race green. [orchestrator-verified: -race green incl. the 5 new tests (cycle-proof symlink, outside-work refusal, suffix parse, double-trash, Esc-preserves-selection)]
